text:
# Linear Expressions — Statement of Outputs

## Overview

*Linear Expressions* by Pat Martino is a four-phase method for jazz guitar improvisation organized entirely around a single governing principle: **harmonic structure dictates melodic choice**. The book-level distillation characterizes the pedagogical arc as moving "from the local to the global" — Phase I establishes horizontal coverage of the neck within a single key, Phase II extends that coverage vertically across all twelve keys, Phase III integrates both axes through line studies anchored to real chord progressions, and Phase IV completes the system by equipping the player to reduce any chord complexity encountered in actual jazz repertoire to a form the earlier phases can handle. Each phase presupposes full mastery of the preceding one; the progression is non-negotiable by design.

The master's fundamental unit is the **activity** — a melodic line form improvised over a harmonic structure. Activities are not free melodic invention: they are positionally and harmonically determined forms derived from the relative major/minor relationship (G minor drawn from Bb major). Five activity forms cover one octave of the fingerboard horizontally, each form matched to a specific chord inversion, making the chord encountered the operative selector for which line form is played. The system is cyclically repeatable because Activities 1 and 5 share the same chord inversion and bookend an octave — a structural symmetry that allows the entire logic to extend without limit up the neck.

The book's most consequential innovation, completing the system in Phase IV, is the principle that **unfamiliar or complex chords are inversions or incomplete versions of familiar diatonic structures**. Rather than treating altered and extended chords as isolated entities requiring separate vocabulary, the master provides a small set of interval-formula substitution rules (minor third up, perfect fifth up, half-step up) that reduce any chord to a simple minor form the activities system already handles. This reductionist logic — combined with the chord substitution equivalences table introduced in Phase III — converts the full complexity of jazz harmony into a closed, executable system grounded in the five activity forms of Chapter 1.

## Systems

### System 1: Chord-to-Activity Positional Mapping System

This is the central system of *Linear Expressions*. The guitar neck is partitioned into five positional scale forms — the five "activities" — derived from the G minor / Bb major relative relationship. Each of the four chord inversions maps to exactly one numbered activity form, so when a player identifies the inversion of the chord they are playing over, that inversion is the complete selector for which activity to use. Activities 1 and 5 share Chord Inversion Form 1 and bookend one octave, making the system cyclically repeatable. Key is determined by position alone, so the entire mapping system transposes to all twelve keys by shifting position. (Chapter 1 summary; Chapter 2 summary)

**Members:** Activity Forms 1–5; Chord Inversion Forms 1–4.

**Traversal Rules:**
- **Chord Inversion Selects Activity Form** — `FamilyCoherence` — Inversion is the selector: Form1 → Forms 1 & 5; Form2 → Form 2; Form3 → Form 3; Form4 → Form 4. ("Each chord form will dictate the activity to be played," p. 10.) (Chapter 1 summary; ch01.md quotes)
- **Horizontal Ascending Position Continuity** — `PositionContinuity` (ascending, span 12 frets, cyclic). ("the five scale patterns move horizontally up the fingerboard, until all twelve frets (one octave) are covered," p. 8.) (Chapter 1 summary; ch01.md quotes)
- **Position Determines Key (Transposition by Shift)** — `PositionContinuity`. ("The first activity starting in the third position is the key of G minor. The first activity starting in the fifth position is the key of A minor," p. 11.) (Chapter 1 summary; ch01.md quotes)
- **Cyclic Octave Wraparound at Activity 5** — `SymmetryMovement`. ("both the fifth and first activities start with the same note on the sixth string … The first and fifth activities share the same chord inversion," p. 15.) (Chapter 1 summary; ch01.md quotes)

**Modification Rules:**
- **Positional Scale Coverage (Non-Root-Based Form)** — `_pending:positional-coverage-form`. ("The scale forms illustrated herein do not necessarily start and end on the root of the scale," p. 8.) (Chapter 1 summary; ch01.md quotes)

### System 2: Harmonic Substitution and Reduction System

This system converts any complex or altered chord into a simple minor form the activities system can handle. Five operative reduction rules — same-quality interchange, relative/secondary-relative minor reduction, m7b5-to-minor, dominant-ninth-to-minor, and altered-dominant step-up — plus the augmented triad's triple enharmonic substitution and a scale-choice preference rule for altered dominants collectively close the gap between the pure G minor world of Phases I–II and the full altered harmony of jazz repertoire. (Chapter 3 summary; Chapter 4 summary; ch03.md quotes; ch04.md quotes)

**Members:** Relative Minor Substitution; Secondary Relative Minor Substitution; Dominant-to-Minor Substitution; m7b5-to-Minor Substitution; Altered Dominant Step-Up Minor Substitution; Augmented Triad as Incomplete Minor-Maj7; Melodic Minor Scale; Harmonic Minor Scale.

**Traversal Rules:**
- **Same-Name/Quality Chord Interchange** — `FamilyCoherence`. ("Substitute chords of the same name and quality," p. 50.) (Chapter 3 summary; ch03.md quotes)

**Modification Rules:**
- **Relative Minor Reduction Rule** — `SubstitutionExpand` (relative_minor; maj6/maj7/maj9). ("Cmaj6 = Am7 (Relative minor)," p. 50.) (Chapter 3 summary; ch03.md quotes)
- **Secondary Relative Minor Fallback Rule** — `SubstitutionExpand` (secondary_relative_minor). ("we go to the secondary relative minor of Db major … namely, F minor," p. 56.) (Chapter 3 & 4 summaries; ch03/ch04.md quotes)
- **m7b5 → Minor via Minor Third Above Root** — `SubstitutionExpand` (minor_third_up). ("From the root of a minor seventh flat five (m7b5) chord, go up a minor third and play minor," p. 55.) (Chapter 4 summary; ch04.md quotes)
- **Dominant Ninth → Minor via Perfect Fifth Above Root** — `SubstitutionExpand` (perfect_fifth_up). ("G9 … up a P5 = Dm6," p. 55.) (Chapter 4 summary; ch04.md quotes)
- **Altered Dominant → Minor One Half-Step Above Root** — `SubstitutionExpand` (half_step_up). ("For Gb7 altered, go up a step and play G minor," p. 57.) (Chapter 4 summary; ch04.md quotes)
- **Augmented Triad → Minor-Maj7 via Major Sixth Above Root** — `SubstitutionExpand` (major_sixth_up; 3 enharmonic roots). ("Over an augmented triad, go up a major 6th from the root and play either melodic or harmonic minor," p. 56.) (Chapter 4 summary; ch04.md quotes)
- **Melodic Minor Preference Over Altered Dominant** — `ColorToneRequire` (required tone b7). ("only the melodic minor contains the b7," p. 57.) (Chapter 4 summary; ch04.md quotes)

### System 3: Line Study Composition and Fingerboard Integration System

Phase III organizes practice and original composition through ten line studies arranged in five ascending pairs (A and B variants). Each successive pair ascends higher on the fingerboard while chord progressions are held invariant by letter designation. Studies carry continuation arrows signaling open-ended cyclical use; the final pair (5A/5B) is intentionally left incomplete to require the student to generate original line content. The terminal application is canonical jazz standards reached through a five-step workflow. (Chapter 3 summary; ch03.md quotes)

**Members:** Line Studies 1A/1B, 2A/2B, 3A/3B, 4A/4B, 5A/5B (Student-Completed); Chord Progression A; Chord Progression B.

**Traversal Rules:**
- **Ascending Pair Progression Up the Fingerboard** — `PositionContinuity`. ("Starting with line studies 2A and 2B, the line studies will ascend up the fingerboard," p. 33.) (Chapter 3 summary; ch03.md quotes)
- **Fixed Chord Progression by Letter Designation** — `FamilyCoherence`. ("Line studies 1A, 2A, 3A, 4A and 5A will share the same chord progression," p. 33.) (Chapter 3 summary; ch03.md quotes)
- **Cyclic Continuation Arrow** — `_pending:cyclic-continuation-marker`. ("you will notice an arrow. This merely suggests that the line study can continue onward," p. 31.) (Chapter 3 summary; ch03.md quotes)

**Modification Rules:**
- **Student Composition Directive (Studies 5A/5B)** — `_pending:student-completion-directive`. ("studies 5A and 5B have been started and left for you to complete," p. 50.) (Chapter 3 summary; ch03.md quotes)
- **Five-Step Substitution-to-Composition Workflow** — `_pending:composition-workflow-sequence` (steps: 5; terminal: jazz_standards). ("1) Study the song in its original form … 5) compose your own solo," p. 50.) (Chapter 3 summary; ch03.md quotes)

## Pending Work

Four `_pending` engine_payload kinds appear in the systems draft:

- **`_pending:positional-coverage-form`** — Signals that activity forms' non-root-anchored, position-complete scale coverage requires a distinct representation type: a positional completeness constraint (display every accessible scale tone in position, regardless of where the root falls in the form). Appears in System 1, modification rule `positional-scale-not-root-based`.
- **`_pending:cyclic-continuation-marker`** — Signals a notational/structural marker (the continuation arrow) that governs looping behavior in performance practice. The arrow is a formal directive embedded in the written studies; its engine encoding requires a cycle/loop construct distinct from the `PositionContinuity` kind used for fingerboard traversal. Appears in System 3, traversal rule `cyclic-continuation`.
- **`_pending:student-completion-directive`** — Signals a compositional obligation imposed on the learner: the rule is that the student must generate content where the book provides none. This is a pedagogy-layer directive (active composition required) rather than a traversal or substitution rule, and has no direct analogue in the standard engine kinds. Appears in System 3, modification rule `student-composition-directive`.
- **`_pending:composition-workflow-sequence`** — Signals an ordered multi-step procedure (five steps, sequentially dependent) applied to the terminal domain of jazz standards. Unlike interval-formula substitution rules, this is a workflow sequence with a defined terminal application, requiring a sequenced-procedure or workflow kind not present in the standard encoding. Appears in System 3, modification rule `five-step-reduction-procedure`.

## Provenance Notes

**Chapter 1** (Phase I, pp. 7–16) is the primary source for System 1 in its entirety. All foundational terminology — "activities," "relative minor," "inversions," "position" — originates here, as do all four traversal rules and the one modification rule of the Chord-to-Activity Positional Mapping System.

**Chapter 2** (Phase II, pp. 17–28) did not yield an independent system. The chapter consists entirely of fingerboard diagrams applying the Chapter 1 rule (chord inversion dictates activity form) vertically across all twelve minor keys (Abm7 through Gm7) in a single fingerboard sector. Coverage is extended; the operative rule is unchanged. Chapter 2's content is absorbed into System 1 (via the `position-determines-key` traversal rule) rather than constituting a separate system. The chapter yielded an empty `quotes[]` in s2 extraction because it contains no load-bearing prose — only notation diagrams.

**Chapter 3** (Phase III, pp. 29–50) yields two systems. Its chord substitution equivalences table contributes the foundational modification rules of System 2 (Harmonic Substitution and Reduction), while its line study framework — ascending pairs, letter-grouped progressions, continuation arrows, student-completed studies, and five-step workflow — constitutes System 3 in full.

**Chapter 4** (Phase IV, pp. 51–63) contributes exclusively to System 2, providing the interval-formula rules for chord types not reachable by relative-minor substitution. No new positional or compositional systems are introduced; the chapter closes the harmonic reduction vocabulary begun in Chapter 3.
